ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for iced honey pomelo lemon tea; closest parent is tea beverage (FOODON_03301099).
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code specific to iced honey pomelo lemon tea; AN000A (tea and tea-based beverages) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 represents tea as a substance; used as closest parent for this flavoured iced tea beverage.

preparation_method: mixed with pomelo pulp and lemon juice

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 350 ml (350 g), equivalent to a large takeaway cup of iced tea as typically served at Malaysian and Singaporean cafes and bistros.

nutrient_source.title: Honey lemon tea, ready-to-drink, estimated from component analysis; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed tea (NDB 14355), honey (NDB 19296), lemon juice (NDB 09152), and pomelo (NDB 09367)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for iced honey pomelo lemon tea as a composite beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis: approximately 80% brewed tea, 8% honey, 7% lemon juice, 5% pomelo juice/pulp, diluted with ice melt. USDA FoodData Central component entries used as the evidence base. Values represent a typical restaurant-style preparation at approximately 6–8% total dissolved solids from honey and fruit.

gi_source.title: Honey, GI value 55 (Atkinson FS et al., 2008); lemon juice GI approximately 20; brewed tea GI effectively 1; composite beverage GI estimated at 40 by weighted average of glycemic carbohydrate contributors
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for iced honey pomelo lemon tea. GI estimated at 40 by weighted contribution of glycemic carbohydrates: honey (GI 55, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008) contributes the dominant glycemic load; lemon juice (GI ~20) and pomelo (GI ~25) contribute minor fractions; brewed tea contributes negligible glycemic carbohydrate. Weighted composite GI calculated as approximately 40, classified as low (≤55). Glycemic load per 350 g serving = (40 × 5.6 × 3.5) / 100 = 7.84, rounded to 7.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (approximately 2 mg per 100 g)
hypertension.guidance: With sodium at approximately 7 mg per 350 ml serving, this beverage poses negligible sodium load and is generally suitable for individuals managing hypertension, though the honey-derived sugar content should be monitored in the context of overall dietary intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Honey-derived free sugars contributing approximately 19 g of sugar per 350 ml serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume this beverage with caution, as the honey content contributes approximately 19 g of free sugars per serving (glycemic load ~7.8), which can elevate postprandial blood glucose; portion control or requesting reduced honey is advisable.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat and cholesterol; antioxidant polyphenols from tea and vitamin C from lemon and pomelo
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: This beverage is low in saturated fat and cholesterol and provides modest antioxidant polyphenols from brewed tea and vitamin C from lemon and pomelo, making it a generally heart-friendly choice when consumed without excessive added honey.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Iced Honey Pomelo Lemon Tea is a chilled, sweetened tea beverage popular in Malaysian and Singaporean cafes and bistros. It is prepared by brewing black or green tea, then combining it with fresh lemon juice, pomelo pulp or juice, and honey as the primary sweetener, served over ice in a large cup. The drink has a refreshing sweet-tart flavour profile with floral honey notes, citrus brightness from lemon, and the mild bittersweet character of pomelo. The texture is light and watery with occasional pomelo pulp pieces. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie beverage at approximately 22 kcal per 100 g, with carbohydrates derived almost entirely from honey and fruit sugars (approximately 5.6 g per 100 g). Fat content is effectively zero (fat_g is naturally zero for this fruit-and-tea-based beverage with no added oils or dairy). Protein is minimal at 0.1 g per 100 g. The beverage provides modest vitamin C from lemon and pomelo, and antioxidant polyphenols from brewed tea. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low GI), with a glycemic load of approximately 7.8 per 350 ml serving, driven primarily by honey. Clinically, it is suitable for most individuals in moderate quantities but should be consumed with caution by those with Type 2 Diabetes due to free sugar content from honey. It is halal, gluten-free, dairy-free, and vegetarian, though not vegan due to honey. It is commonly found on the menus of Malaysian-style cafes, kopitiam-inspired bistros, and bubble tea shops across Singapore.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for iced honey pomelo lemon tea as a composite beverage. Nutrient values are estimated by weighted component analysis using USDA FoodData Central entries for brewed tea, honey, lemon juice, and pomelo. GI is estimated from University of Sydney GI Database values for honey and fruit components. Confidence is medium because the estimation methodology is defensible and documented, but direct measurement data for this specific beverage is not available in any approved primary source.
notes: Fat_g is naturally zero for this fruit-and-tea-based beverage containing no added oils, cream, or dairy. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and cholesterol_mg are all confirmed zero by product formulation (no fat-containing ingredients). Protein_g at 0.1 g per 100 g reflects trace protein from honey and tea solids; this is not zero. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA component sources for this beverage type. Vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg is -1 as not reported in the USDA component sources. Vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg is -1 as not reported. Vitamin_b3_niacin_mg is -1 as not reported. Vitamin_b6_mg is -1 as not reported. Vitamin_b12_mcg is -1 as naturally zero for plant-based beverages but not explicitly confirmed per serving; set to -1 per sentinel convention. Folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as not reported in the USDA component sources for this beverage. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as naturally zero for this beverage and not reported. Vitamin_e_mg is -1 as not reported. Phosphorus_mg is -1 as not reported in the USDA component sources. Magnesium_mg is -1 as not reported. Zinc_mg is -1 as not reported. Selenium_mcg is -1 as not reported. Glycemic index set to 40 (low GI, ≤55) estimated from weighted honey and fruit sugar contributions per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Glycemic load per 350 g serving = 7.8. This beverage is served at The Rainbow Bistro (60 Boat Quay, Singapore) and is classified as Malaysian cuisine. The cuisine tags Curry Rice and Nasi Lemak from the source record are restaurant-level tags and do not apply to this beverage item specifically.
